items.q: Does the halting problem mean we can never tell whether any program halts?
items.correct: No - many individual programs can easily be proven to halt or loop; the theorem says no single GENERAL algorithm works for all programs and inputs.
items.misconception: Yes - it is impossible to know if any program will ever finish.
items.random_wrong: Yes - the halting problem says all programs run forever.
items.why: The universal quantifier is dropped in memory. Undecidability is about one algorithm covering every case, not about our inability to analyse specific programs.

items.q: Can a finite state machine count an unbounded number of items, such as matching nested brackets?
items.correct: No - an FSM's only memory is its current state, so with finitely many states it cannot track unbounded counts; that needs a stack or more powerful model.
items.misconception: Yes - an FSM can remember any amount of information in its transitions.
items.random_wrong: Yes - adding more arrows gives an FSM unlimited memory.
items.why: Transitions look like memory. Each extra count needs a distinguishable configuration, and a finite machine runs out - the classic argument placing FSMs below pushdown automata.

items.q: Is a Turing machine a physical computer that was built in the 1930s?
items.correct: No - it is an abstract mathematical model used to define what is computable; its infinite tape means it cannot be fully built, only simulated.
items.misconception: Yes - Turing machines were the first commercially sold computers.
items.random_wrong: Yes - a Turing machine is another name for the Bombe.
items.why: Turing's wartime hardware fame fuses with his theory. The machine is a thought experiment; its power is definitional, anchoring the Church-Turing thesis.

items.q: A finite state machine consists of:
items.correct: A finite set of states with transitions between them triggered by inputs
items.misconception: An infinite tape and a read/write head
items.why: It's A finite set of states with transitions between them triggered by inputs. “An infinite tape and a read/write head” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A Turing machine includes which components?
items.correct: An infinite tape, a read/write head, a state register and transition rules
items.random_wrong: A finite tape that cannot be written to
items.why: Correct answer: An infinite tape, a read/write head, a state register and transition rules. “A monitor, mouse and keyboard” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: No general algorithm can decide whether every program halts on every input
items.why: The answer examiners want is: No general algorithm can decide whether every program halts on every input. “All programs eventually stop” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A universal Turing machine is special because it can:
items.correct: Simulate any other Turing machine from a description on its tape
items.why: It's Simulate any other Turing machine from a description on its tape. “Solve the halting problem” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An FSM has 3 states and a binary input alphabet, with exactly one transition per state per input symbol. How many transitions does it have?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 transitions. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
